behaviors.description: Required patient fields validated before downstream processing; missing fields produce a clear error listing all missing items.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is entirely within a single validation function's logic — whether it collects all missing fields before returning. No module handoff is involved. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess needed. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML. MULTI-STEP: No. A plain unit test calling the validator with incomplete input is sufficient to catch every real failure mode here.
behaviors.test_description: Call the patient intake validator with inputs that omit each required field individually and in combination (name, DOB, insurance ID). Assert that the returned error lists exactly the missing field names in every case, and that a fully populated input passes without error.

behaviors.description: Date of birth validation rejects future dates, dates more than 150 years ago, and non-date strings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Three distinct boundary conditions on a single parsing/validation function. The failure appears entirely within that function's logic — no module handoff involved. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: None. This is a parameterized unit test over the DOB validator boundary cases.
behaviors.test_description: Parameterize the DOB validator with: today+1 day (future), today-150 years-1 day (too old), non-date strings (e.g., 'abc', ''), and valid boundary dates just inside the acceptable range. Assert rejection/acceptance respectively, and that the error message is clear for each rejection case.

behaviors.description: Insurance eligibility check sends the correct patient ID and plan code to the payer API and correctly interprets 'eligible', 'not eligible', and 'pending' responses.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior has two distinct failure modes: (a) the HTTP request is assembled incorrectly (wrong fields), and (b) the response-to-domain-status mapping is wrong. Both require the insurance service module to wire to an HTTP client — the failure only appears at that module boundary. REAL-INFRA: A stub HTTP server (e.g., httpretty or a local test server) can capture the exact payload sent and return controlled responses, so real network is not needed. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) stub HTTP suffices — not System; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-step. Integration is the floor.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the insurance eligibility service to a stub HTTP server. Submit requests for three scenarios: eligible, not-eligible, and pending. Assert that each outbound request contains the correct patient ID and plan code fields. Assert that the service maps each response body to the correct domain status enum value.

behaviors.description: When the payer API is unreachable, the system marks eligibility as 'pending' and continues the intake rather than blocking.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode — does the system propagate a 'pending' status to downstream steps instead of raising/blocking — only appears when the insurance module hands off error state to the intake orchestration layer. A Unit test of the insurance module alone cannot verify that intake continues. REAL-INFRA: The unreachable-API condition can be simulated by configuring the stub HTTP client to raise a connection error; real network is not needed. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real network needed; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-step agentic chain.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the HTTP client dependency to raise a connection timeout. Run the intake orchestration through the insurance step. Assert that the eligibility field on the intake record is set to 'pending', and that control passes to the next step (triage/scheduling) without an exception being surfaced to the caller.

behaviors.description: AI symptom triage correctly assigns high urgency to symptom combinations that indicate emergency conditions (e.g., chest pain + shortness of breath).
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Correctly assigns' is a claim about classification quality from a real AI medical reasoning engine. A deterministic stub would always return whatever the test author hard-codes, making the test vacuously pass regardless of model quality. The real failure — the model misclassifying a life-threatening symptom combination — only surfaces with the real model. MOCK-HIDE: Confirmed. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: no behavior text states the engine returns a deterministic, non-ML response; the description explicitly identifies it as an AI medical reasoning engine.
behaviors.test_description: Submit the symptom combination 'chest pain + shortness of breath' to the real AI medical reasoning engine via the triage service. Assert that the returned urgency level is 'high'. Optionally parametrize with additional known-emergency combinations (e.g., 'sudden severe headache + vision loss') to increase signal.

behaviors.description: Symptom triage produces a natural language explanation of its urgency assessment that a clinician can review, not just a numeric score.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Whether the triage response is a coherent, clinician-readable natural language explanation (rather than a bare number) depends on what the real AI engine generates. A stub trivially returns a pre-canned string. The real failure — the engine returning only a score, a malformed string, or a non-explanation — only surfaces with the real model. MOCK-HIDE: Confirmed. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: no text disqualifies dependence on real generation quality.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real AI triage engine with a representative symptom set. Assert that the response object contains a non-empty string explanation field (not just a numeric field), and that the explanation text references the submitted symptoms or their clinical significance — not merely a constant template.

behaviors.description: Appointment is scheduled in the first available slot matching the assigned urgency level — high urgency gets same-day, medium gets within 48 hours.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure appears when the scheduling service queries the calendar database for available slots and applies urgency-window filtering. Correctness depends on the service-to-DB handoff (wrong query, wrong time window calculation, wrong slot selection). REAL-INFRA: A local in-process test database (or an in-memory equivalent with real query semantics) can reproduce the failure. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real network needed; (b) urgency level is a deterministic input to this step, no AI/ML; (c) no multi-step chain.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a test calendar database with known slot availability. Invoke the scheduling service with urgency='high' and assert the booked slot falls within today. Repeat with urgency='medium' and assert the booked slot is within 48 hours. Assert that the earliest qualifying slot is chosen, not an arbitrary one.

behaviors.description: Scheduling does not double-book a time slot even when two patients are being processed concurrently.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is a race condition at the database transaction boundary — two concurrent writers both read 'slot available' before either commits. An in-memory fake without real transaction/locking semantics will not reproduce this. REAL-INFRA: Requires a real local database instance with proper transaction isolation (e.g., a local PostgreSQL or SQLite with WAL mode), but not a remote/external service. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) a local DB process with real locking semantics is needed — this is Integration not System because no external network/OS resource is required beyond a local DB; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real local database, launch two concurrent scheduling coroutines/threads targeting the same single available slot. Assert that exactly one booking succeeds and the other receives a 'slot unavailable' or conflict error. Assert the slot record has exactly one booking afterward.

behaviors.description: Confirmation message includes patient name, appointment time, provider name, and preparation instructions appropriate to the visit type.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode — missing or incorrect preparation instructions — depends on the confirmation generator querying visit-type data to look up appropriate instructions and composing it with appointment/provider data. That lookup is a module boundary (confirmation service → visit-type data store). REAL-INFRA: A local test fixture or in-memory data store covers the lookup. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) no real network; (b) no AI/ML; (c) no multi-step agentic chain. REJECTION-GATE for Unit: 'appropriate to the visit type' implies a data-driven lookup beyond pure string templating, so Unit is too low.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a test visit-type data store with known preparation instructions for two visit types. Invoke the confirmation generator with a completed appointment record (patient name, time, provider). Assert the output string contains all four required fields and that the preparation instructions match the expected text for the given visit type.

behaviors.description: Full intake pipeline — validate, check insurance, triage symptoms, schedule, confirm — completes for a real patient submission without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly chains all five pipeline stages end-to-end, including an AI triage step. LLM-DEP: The triage stage uses a real AI medical reasoning engine whose output drives the scheduling step. The combination of multiple agentic steps in sequence — where the output of real AI triage determines scheduling, which determines confirmation — is the definition of Workflow. An Integration test cannot verify that AI-generated urgency correctly flows into downstream scheduling; an Agentic test covers only the triage step in isolation.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a complete patient intake payload (valid name, DOB, insurance ID, and a symptom set) to the full pipeline with real external dependencies (payer API sandbox or stub, real AI triage engine, real local calendar DB, real confirmation service). Assert that the pipeline returns a completed intake record with all five stages marked done, an urgency level assigned, an appointment booked in a slot consistent with that urgency, and a confirmation message containing all required fields.
